Databricks has made GPT-5.5 available for enterprise agent workflows after the model achieved a new high on OfficeQA Pro, the company's benchmark for complex document processing tasks. The milestone matters because parsing legacy documents and scanned PDFs reliably remains a production bottleneck for AI agents—small extraction errors in these workflows compound downstream, breaking entire task chains. GPT-5.5 reduced errors by 46% compared to GPT-5.4 and became the first model to exceed 50% accuracy on the benchmark.

## Performance gains in document parsing

OfficeQA Pro evaluates models on tasks involving scanned PDFs, legacy files, and long-context documents—the kinds of materials that frequently cause agent systems to fail in real deployments. Databricks research engineer Arnav Singhvi attributed the largest gains to improvements in parsing older documents. "Earlier models like 5.4 were unable to parse all the digits correctly, but it seems like 5.5 has a step-function lift in parsing older documents and scanned PDFs," he said. The benchmark also includes multi-step retrieval and reasoning workflows, where GPT-5.5 showed reduced tendency to pursue unnecessary search detours that earlier versions would take.

## Production implications for agent systems

The results signal a meaningful capability shift for enterprise AI deployments. Databricks offers GPT-5.5 through its AI Unity Gateway, integrated with AgentBricks and the Agent Supervisor API—systems where the model orchestrates parsing, retrieval, and execution across specialized agents. For practitioners building custom agent workflows, this means a more reliable foundation for document-heavy tasks without additional supervision. The capability is particularly relevant for financial services, insurance, and legal technology workflows where document parsing errors directly impact downstream business logic. Organizations relying on agents to extract data from scanned forms, contracts, or legacy databases should expect measurable improvements in end-to-end accuracy.
items.original_title: Databricks brings GPT-5.5 to enterprise agent workflows

items.headline: OpenAI Details How Codex Consolidates Sales Data Into Deal Documents
items.body:
OpenAI has published guidance on how sales teams can use Codex to synthesize scattered account information—CRM records, call transcripts, email threads, Slack discussions, and usage signals—into structured working documents. The approach treats AI as a document assembly tool that produces first drafts of pipeline briefs, meeting prep packets, forecast reviews, and account plans, with sales professionals retaining final judgment on strategy and next steps.

## Three Core Sales Workflows

OpenAI identifies three primary use cases. Pipeline prioritization takes underworked accounts and ranks them by trigger, pain point, stakeholder access, and urgency, returning a prioritized brief with outreach sequences and CRM-ready next steps. Meeting prep and follow-up uses calendar context and account history to generate pre-meeting briefs, then converts post-meeting notes or transcripts into customer follow-ups, internal recaps, and CRM updates. Forecast review aggregates opportunity records, deal threads, call notes, and usage signals to surface which deals should remain in commit, move to upside, or be pulled entirely.

The guidance includes starter prompts and real-world examples. For pipeline prioritization, users provide Codex with account lists, CRM exports, call transcripts, and usage signals, then ask it to rank accounts and create a stakeholder map. For meeting prep, users supply calendar context, prior calls, and account materials; Codex returns a brief with customer priorities and likely questions. For forecasting, users feed forecast snapshots and opportunity details; Codex surfaces sourced facts versus inferred risks with deal-by-deal rationale.

## Implications for Sales Operations

The framing reflects a practical view of AI's role in sales: aggregating context that already exists but lives across fragmented systems. Codex does not generate strategy; it pulls together evidence and produces a structured summary that sales teams can pressure-test and refine. This distinction—between drafting and deciding—preserves accountability while reducing the friction of manually collating information from email, Slack, spreadsheets, and call recordings.

For enterprise sales organizations, the immediate value lies in speed and consistency. A sales manager can generate forecast risk reviews in minutes rather than hours spent consolidating emails and CRM notes. A seller can prepare for a customer meeting by asking Codex to surface open issues and stakeholder context from prior conversations. The suggested plugin integrations—Gmail, Slack, Gong, Google Drive—indicate that the tool expects to pull directly from existing enterprise workflows rather than requiring data export and reformatting.

The practical take-away: Codex is positioned as a consolidation layer for sales teams drowning in context spread across multiple tools. Success depends on data quality and access permissions, not on the AI generating novel insight. Organizations considering deployment should focus on standardizing which sources Codex pulls from and establishing guidelines for when human review is required before a document reaches a customer or leadership review.
items.original_title: How sales teams use Codex
